exemplar.title: How does the mass of a magnet affect its drop time through a copper tube?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the mass of a magnet affect its drop time through a copper tube?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables include units, significant figures, and absolute uncertainties, with clear sample calculations.
Graphs are properly labelled, captioned, and include error bars derived from propagated uncertainties.
Processing steps (averages, log transforms, gradient determination) are communicated clearly and precisely.
weaknesses: Inconsistent significant-figure notation in some table cells (e.g., raw times vs uncertainties).
Omission of systematic uncertainties from tube length, magnetic field strength, and mass in final error analysis.
Use of average of max/min gradients instead of least-squares fitting introduces bias.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data and processing is both clear and precise, meriting the top of the 3-4 band edging into 5. Consideration of uncertainties is appropriate but omits some systematic sources. Data processing is mostly accurate with minor methodological inaccuracies. A score of 5 balances these high-quality elements with small omissions.

strengths: Research question is clearly stated and embedded in a specific context with links to Lenzâs and Faradayâs laws and everyday electromagnetic applications.
Apparatus table includes dimensions with uncertainties and sensor details, enabling reproducibility.
Step-by-step procedure and labelled diagram allow another student to largely reproduce the method.
weaknesses: No description of the exact timing synchronization mechanism (trigger vs verbal cue) reducing full reproducibility.
Derivation of magnetic flux equation is omitted, limiting theoretical completeness.
Assumption of perpendicular field (Î¸=0Â°) is not justified with reference to measurements or simulations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research design excels in context (band 5-6) and provides detailed apparatus and controls, justifying a high mark. Methodological description and considerations are in the 3-4 bandâsufficiently reproducible but with ambiguitiesâso a holistic score of 5 reflects strong performance overall while recognizing minor omissions.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly states the inverse relationship between drop time and mass, consistent with processed data.
References to external studies (Knight et al., Serway & Jewett) show engagement with accepted scientific context.
weaknesses: Discussion of literature comparison is qualitative and brief, lacking specific exponent values for deeper justification.
No quantitative reconciliation of the observed exponent (â3) with the theoretical expectation (â1).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully consistent with analysis and supported by data (3-4 band). The scientific context comparison is relevant but remains superficial, placing it at the lower end of the band. A score of 4 reflects solid consistency and support with room for deeper quantitative context.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (reaction time, magnet oscillations, terminal velocity assumption) and discusses their impacts.
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (light gates, guide rails, automated release) linked clearly to each identified weakness.
Additional suggestions (testing different materials, mapping field strength) demonstrate depth and forward planning.
weaknesses: Does not rank which methodological limitation most affected the results, missing an opportunity to quantify relative impact.
Lacks discussion of feasibility or resource trade-offs for suggested improvements (e.g., cost vs benefit of light gates).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation explains the impact of specific weaknesses and provides realistic, well-explained improvements, meeting the 5-6 band for suggestions. Methodological weakness explanations fit the upper 3-4 band. Overall, a score of 5 rewards comprehensive evaluation and actionable recommendations while noting minor depth issues.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated and immediately embedded in a clear, focused context: the fall of a magnet through a 2 m copper tube to explore Lenz/Faraday effects.  The background narrative links the phenomenon to everyday electromagnetic technologies and explains why drop-time matters, demonstrating a specific and appropriate context rather than a broad or generic one.  Minor omissions (e.g. no mention of how the results could be applied quantitatively in engineering) prevent a flawless 6, but the specificity and relevance comfortably meet the 5-6 band.

Random uncertainties are calculated for each mean, propagated into logarithms, and used to generate error bars and gradient uncertainty.  The student qualitatively discusses systematic effects later.  Some omissions remain (no uncertainty for tube length, magnetic field, or mass in final analysis), so consideration is appropriate but not fully comprehensive, matching the 3-4 bandâs upper level.

The student identifies controls (tube dimensions, orientation, drop height, magnet strength) and justifies each.  Preliminary testing to choose tube/magnet sizes shows awareness of sufficiency.  However, explanations of why five trials are enough, why a 1 g mass increment is optimal, or how large uncertainties influence data sufficiency are brief.  Considerations are described but not fully explained, placing the work solidly in the 3-4 band, nearer 3 than 4.

Specific weaknessesâhuman reaction time, magnet oscillations, limited mass range, assumption of instant terminal velocityâare identified and their impacts on gradient uncertainty and validity are discussed, though depth varies.  Impact explanations move the strand beyond simple description but stop short of ranking or quantifying relative magnitude, so it aligns with the 3-4 band.

Apparatus tables list dimensions with uncertainties, masses, and sensor details; step-by-step instructions and a labelled diagram mean another student could largely reproduce the work.  Ambiguities remain (no description of the exact release mechanism or how the stopwatch operator synchronises start/stop), so replication would still require judgment.  Thus the description is "reproducible with few ambiguities," fitting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Averages, logarithms, gradient, RMSE, and propagated uncertainties are calculated consistently and plotted.  The mathematics are mostly correct, though averaging max/min gradients rather than using least-squares introduces inaccuracy, and the derived exponent is mis-interpreted theoretically.  These issues constitute âsome significant inaccuracies,â so the strand sits in the middle 3-4 band.

Improvements are not only realistic (light gates, guide rails, automated release) but also clearly linked to the stated weaknesses with explanations of how each would reduce specific uncertainties or extend validity (e.g. Â±0.001 s timing precision).  Additional suggestionsâdifferent materials, mapping field strengthâextend the investigation.  The explanatory depth meets the 5-6 band; minor missing detail on feasibility keeps the score at 5.

Data tables include units, significant figures, and absolute uncertainties; graphs are labelled and accompanied by captions.  Processing steps (averages, log transforms, gradient determination) are shown with sample calculations.  Communication is both clear and precise overall; minor lapses (e.g. inconsistent significant-figure notation in a few cells) prevent a perfect 6.

References to Knight et al. and Serway & Jewett show comparison with accepted physics literature, and the student contrasts their â3 exponent with a theoretical â1.  However, the discussion remains qualitative and brief, lacking a deeper quantitative reconciliation.  This is âsome relevant comparison,â meriting a 3 within the 3-4 band.

The student states that drop time decreases with mass, quoting numerical ranges and linking to the log-log gradient; this is consistent with the processed data.  They acknowledge large gradient uncertainty and limited mass range, displaying awareness of limitations.  The conclusion is therefore described and largely consistent, satisfying the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The controlled-variables table clearly explains why each factor is held constant, demonstrating thoughtful methodological considerations.

In Table 1 of quantitative data, times are given to one decimal place but uncertainties are Â±0.2 s; consider using consistent significant figures and report raw times to two decimals if your stopwatch allows.
The propagation of uncertainties into log plots is executed correctly, but omission of tube-length uncertainty neglects a systematic source; include Â±0.001 m in final error bars.

The research question is clearly stated and embedded within a specific context by linking the fall of a magnet through a copper tube to Lenzâs and Faradayâs laws, demonstrating relevance to everyday electromagnetic technologies.
Including the word count here is administrative; consider removing it from the main body to maintain academic focus and place administrative details in a cover sheet.
The introduction effectively situates the research question, but it would benefit from a brief statement on how the quantified results could inform real-world engineering design applications.
The background correctly explains Faradayâs and Lenzâs laws, but the derivation of magnetic flux (Equation 2) is omitted and should be explicitly presented for completeness.
In the derivation section, the assumption that the magnetâs field remains perpendicular (Î¸=0Â°) should be justified with a reference to field uniformity measurements or simulations.
The methodological description omits the exact mechanism for synchronizing start/stop of timing; specify whether a trigger system or verbal cue was used to improve reproducibility.
The apparatus table is detailed and includes uncertainties, but the electronic scaleâs precision is not specified; add its Â± value to ensure clarity in mass measurements.
The choice of seven mass increments is reasonable, but the justification for five trials per mass could be strengthened by a power-analysis estimate of required sample size.
The uncertainty in the gradient is calculated but rounded to Â±2, which obscures the true precision; consider reporting Îm to one decimal place to reflect propagation.
The comparison to past studies is relevant but lacks quantitative detail; include specific exponent values from Knight et al. or Serway & Jewett to justify the theoretical context.
The evaluation uniquely identifies time-reaction and oscillation limitations, but lacks ranking of which limitation most significantly affected the gradient uncertainty.
Average and log transformations are communicated with clarity and precision, complete with sample calculations that guide the reader through each processing step.
The linear fit and RMSE calculation are commendable, but using average of max/min gradients rather than least-squares fitting introduces bias; apply regression software for consistency.
The conclusion correctly states an inverse relationship and references numerical ranges, showing consistency with processed data.
Suggested improvements are realistic and well-linked to identified weaknesses; for deeper evaluation, explain trade-offs or resource requirements of implementing light gates or guide rails.
The suggestion to test different tube materials and map field strength is valuable; enhance the proposal by outlining specific measurement methods (e.g., thermal sensors for tube temperature).

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: How does current affect force on an insulated copper wire in a magnetic field?

userSubmission.researchQuestion: How does current affect force on an insulated copper wire in a magnetic field?

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
